pathogens: Invades intestinal epithelium, disrupts mucosal barrier and triggers inflammatory diarrhea.
Enterotoxin that damages intestinal epithelium, increases permeability and drives inflammatory diarrhea.
Potent cytotoxin that disrupts epithelial tight junctions and causes severe colonic inflammation.
Produces Shiga toxins and endotoxin (LPS) that damage intestinal lining and can cause hemorrhagic colitis.
Highly virulent EHEC strain producing Shiga toxins linked to bloody diarrhea and kidney complications.
Invades intestinal epithelial cells causing tissue destruction, inflammation and dysentery-like symptoms.
Produces heat-labile and heat-stable enterotoxins that stimulate intestinal fluid secretion leading to watery diarrhea.
Releases potent Shiga toxins that inhibit host protein synthesis and damage intestinal and vascular tissues.
Invades intestinal mucosa and Peyer's patches, triggering strong inflammatory immune responses via endotoxin (LPS)
Produces cholera toxin that drives massive electrolyte and water secretion from the intestine.
Invades lymphoid tissues of the gut and can mimic inflammatory bowel disease symptoms.
Colonizes gastric mucosa, alters acid balance and promotes chronic inflammation affecting the upper GI environment.
dysbiotic_overgrowth_bacteria: Spore-forming environmental bacteria that can modulate microbiome balance; overgrowth may reflect microbial imbalance or transient colonization
Spore-forming environmental bacteria that can modulate microbiome balance; overgrowth may reflect microbial imbalance or transient colonization
Gut commensal capable of biofilm formation and antimicrobial resistance; elevated levels may indicate dysbiosis and opportunistic infection risk.
Gut commensal capable of biofilm formation and antimicrobial resistance; elevated levels may indicate dysbiosis and opportunistic infection risk.
Environmental opportunistic bacteria capable of toxin production and biofilm formation, often emerging in disrupted microbiomes.
Environmental opportunistic bacteria capable of toxin production and biofilm formation, often emerging in disrupted microbiomes.
Sulfite-reducing bacterium associated with bile acid metabolism and inflammatory responses when enriched in the gut microbiome.
Opportunistic Gram-positive bacteria that can colonize the gut during dysbiosis and contribute to toxin production and inflammatory responses.
Opportunistic pathogen capable of toxin production and epithelial barrier disruption; intestinal colonization may occur when normal microbiota are depleted.
Lactic acid-producing commensals that can expand during microbial imbalance; elevated levels are associated with dysbiosis and inflammatory gut conditions.
commensal_overgrowth_inflammatory_relate: Sulfate-reducing bacteria that produce hydrogen sulfide, which can damage intestinal epithelium and promote inflammation when overgrown.
Methane-producing archaea that influence gut fermentation and motility; elevated levels are linked with slower intestinal transit.
Opportunistic Enterobacteriaceae that may expand during dysbiosis and contribute to gut inflammation and microbial imbalance through endotoxin (LPS) production
Opportunistic Gram-negative bacterium associated with intestinal inflammation and dysbiosis when present in elevated amounts.
Opportunistic Enterobacteriaceae capable of endotoxin (LPS) production; expansion is associated with inflammatory gut conditions.
Pathobiont capable of mucosal colonization and inflammatory activation, particularly during microbiome disruption.
Environmental opportunistic bacterium occasionally colonizing the gut and associated with chronic intestinal inflammation in susceptible hosts.
Urease-producing opportunistic bacteria that may contribute to intestinal dysbiosis and inflammatory signaling when overrepresented.
Opportunistic pathobiont producing endotoxins (LPS) and virulence factors that may disrupt gut microbial balance.
Anaerobic bacteria linked with inflammatory signaling and mucosal barrier disruption when enriched in the gut microbiome.
Fiber-associated commensals involved in carbohydrate fermentation; elevated levels may reflect diet-driven shifts or inflammatory microbiome states.
